title: Modular Office Narrow Drawer Base Unit
summary: A narrow two-drawer plywood pedestal designed as one interchangeable module in a modular office system — genuinely small enough to slot under a desktop alongside other matching base units.
description:
This is a single module meant to sit alongside other matching bases under a shared desktop, rather than a standalone desk — a narrow plywood pedestal holding two drawers, sized to be genuinely interchangeable with other units in the same modular system. **If you want to finish the front edges of the plywood with edge banding, do that before assembly, not after** — the plan specifically calls out doing this early since only the side front edges need it, and it's far easier to iron on banding to a flat panel than to a partially assembled cabinet.

Attach the 1x boards to the plywood sides with pocket holes if you have a jig, or self-tapping screws and glue if you don't — clamping the boards down while attaching helps if you're working solo without an extra set of hands. The top attaches with brad nails and glue, all outside edges kept flush.

**Drawer depth is a genuine build-time choice**: use 1x10s instead of the plan's 1x8s for taller drawer sides, or 1x6s to save a little money at the cost of shorter sides — pick your depth before cutting rather than mixing sizes within one drawer. Install the drawer members on the slides with the drawer box inset 3/4" from the cabinet's front edge, test the fit before final attachment, and adjust now if anything binds — this is much easier to fix before the drawer face goes on than after.

tools.note: Holding boards in place while attaching solo.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/2" strips, half sheet or 8 ft strip
Pocket hole screws, 1-1/4", or self-tapping wood screws, 2"
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.material: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/2" strips, half sheet or 8 ft strip
3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/2" strips, half sheet or 8 ft strip

cutList.note: Cut to fit; positioned in a later step.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: With the 3/4" plywood already ripped to 15 1/2" wide, cut two sides 29" long and the top 19 1/2" long. From the 1x4, cut two base trims 18"; from the 1x2, four top and middle trims 18". Those 18" pieces span between the two 3/4" plywood sides, which makes the cabinet 19 1/2" wide overall - exactly the length of the top, so nothing needs trimming later. From the two 8 ft 1x8s, cut four drawer sides 14" and four drawer fronts and backs 15 1/2"; cut the 1/4" plywood drawer bottoms 17" by 14". Leave the 1x12 drawer-face stock full length for now, because the faces get cut to the openings you actually end up with.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/2" strips, half sheet or 8 ft strip

steps.title: Edge band and assemble the sides
steps.body: If you want the plywood's front edges finished, iron the banding onto the two 29" sides now, while they are flat, separate boards; only those front edges need it, and doing it after assembly means working an iron into a corner. Then join the two sides with the 1x boards: the two 18" 1x4 base trims at the bottom and the four 18" 1x2 trims at the top and at the drawer divide, all set between the sides with their faces flush to the plywood's front and back edges. Use 3/4"-setting pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, or 2" self-tapping screws and glue, clamping each board while you drive it if you are working solo. Measure the diagonals across the front before the last board goes in, because the cabinet has to be square or the Euro slides will bind.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/2" strips, half sheet or 8 ft strip
Pocket hole screws, 1-1/4", or self-tapping wood screws, 2"

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Set the 19 1/2" x 15 1/2" plywood top over the cabinet with all outside edges flush; its 19 1/2" length is the cabinet's exact outside width, so it should not overhang anywhere. Glue every edge it lands on and nail it down with 1-1/4" brad nails into the sides and the top trims. Sight along the top edge to check it is flat before the glue sets - this module is meant to sit under a shared desktop alongside other bases, and a bowed top will rock whatever goes above it.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/2" strips, half sheet or 8 ft strip

steps.title: Build the drawers
steps.body: Build each drawer box with the 15 1/2" fronts and backs between the two 14" sides. That puts the box at 17" wide by 14" deep, which matches the 17" x 14" plywood bottoms exactly and leaves 1/2" of clearance on each side inside the 18" cabinet opening for the Euro slides. Join with 3/4"-setting pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, or 1-1/4" brad nails and glue, and check each box across its diagonals before the glue sets. Attach the 1/4" bottoms with 3/4" brad nails and glue, since the bottom is what holds the box square once it is on. Decide the drawer depth before cutting: the 1x8 in the cut list gives the sides as listed, 1x10 makes them deeper and 1x6 shallower, but never mix sizes within one drawer.
materials: Pocket hole screws, 1-1/4", or self-tapping wood screws, 2"

steps.title: Install the drawer slides
steps.body: Mount the drawer members of the slides to the bottom of each box and the cabinet members inside the case, level and parallel to the cabinet front. Set each box in so its front sits 3/4" back from the front edge of the cabinet, which is the space the applied face will take up. Get both pairs at exactly the same distance back and the same distance apart on each side; Euro bottom-mount slides need that 1/2" per side and will bind on anything less.

steps.title: Test fit and adjust
steps.body: Run each drawer all the way in and out several times and watch where it catches. A drawer rubbing on one side usually means the cabinet is out of square rather than the slide being wrong; a drawer that drops at the front means the two slides are not at the same height. Fix it now, because with the faces off and the cabinet open you can still reach every screw, and once the faces are on you cannot.

steps.title: Attach the drawer face
steps.body: Stand the cabinet upright and cut the faces from the 1x12 to your actual openings, leaving an even gap around each; mark and cut them together so the pair matches. Hold each face in position with shims or a coin as a spacer, check the gaps top and bottom, then tack it with a few 1-1/4" brad nails driven where no slide screw or hardware hole will later land. Pull the drawer out and drive a couple of screws from inside the box into the back of the face once you are happy with it, because brad nails alone will not hold a face over time.
materials: Pocket hole screws, 1-1/4", or self-tapping wood screws, 2"

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Fill the nail holes and any exposed plywood voids, then sand every surface, paying particular attention to the plywood edges you did not band, since those are what a hand meets reaching for a drawer. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly. Finish the top the same as the rest even if a desktop will cover it, so the module can be moved to a different position in the system later without looking out of place.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Narrow two-drawer modular office base unit
